report.cost_of_living: Tokyo has high living costs compared to other Japanese cities. Monthly expenses for a single student typically range from ¥130,000–¥200,000 ($920–$1,410 USD), including rent, food, and commuting. Rent alone varies from ¥40,000–¥80,000 depending on the accommodation type. Scholarships and affordable dormitories often exist, but students must plan meticulously for initial expenses. [University Living](https://www.universityliving.com/blog) [YOLO JAPAN](https://www.yolo-japan.com)
report.academic: Japan's computer science programs offer research-oriented curricula with ties to industries like AI, robotics, and software development. The Tokyo Institute of Technology stands out nationally, but language barriers can limit access to resources for non-fluent Japanese speakers. English is increasingly common at top-tier universities. [Tokyo Tech Admissions](https://www.titech.ac.jp/english/admissions) [JASSO](https://www.studyinjapan.go.jp)
report.visa: Undergraduate students require a Student Visa, which allows part-time work up to 28 hours/week. It is valid for the program duration plus 3 months. Visa renewal and compliance require financial proof and academic engagement. [Immigration Official Website](https://www.mofa.go.jp/j_info/visit/visa)
report.culture_shock: Tokyo offers a mix of cosmopolitan life and traditional Japanese customs. International students often face isolation due to group-based university culture and language barriers in daily interactions. However, cultural immersion programs, clubs, and events at universities can help bridge gaps. [Tokyo University Guide](https://www.u-tokyo.ac.jp)
report.community: International student networks like JASSO provide peer mentors and tutors. Tokyo hosts numerous cultural exchange programs, but integration into academic or social circles can still be limited for non-Japanese speakers. [JASSO](https://www.jasso.go.jp)
report.career: Tokyo provides robust career opportunities in tech, including internships at companies like Rakuten or Panasonic. Entry-level salaries for Computer Science grads range between ¥3M–¥6M/year, with higher rates at multinational firms for skilled developers. [Tokyo Tech Career Resources](https://www.titech.ac.jp/english/admissions/prospective-students/career-paths)
report.safety: Tokyo is one of the safest cities globally, with low crime rates and well-lit, secure public areas. Students should still avoid walking alone at night in less populous areas. Emergency hotlines (110 for police, 119 for fire/medical) are ready and efficient. [Tokyo Cheapo](https://tokyocheapo.com) [Safety Official Info](https://www.u-tokyo.ac.jp/adm/inbound/en/life-safety-info.html)
report.climate: Tokyo's humid summers and cold winters can be overwhelming without preparation. Summers average 30°C (86°F), but air conditioning is common. Winters drop to 5°C (41°F) but rarely hit freezing. [Global Livability Index](https://www.globalliveabilityindex.com/tokyo-liveability-guide)
report.part_time_work: Student visas permit up to 28 hours/week in roles like tutoring or retail, with wages ranging between ¥1,000–¥1,500/hour. Prohibited sectors include nightlife-related establishments. [Study-Abroad Works](https://www.study-abroad.org/study-in-japan/work-and-career)

health: Humid summers leading to heat fatigue (air conditioning available).
Secure public streets with well-lit paths reduce crime-related stress.
Navigating local clinics without Japanese fluency may be a challenge.
Commuting 1-2 hours daily can cause physical exhaustion.
Accessible clean tap water and healthy dining options improve well-being.
mood: High loneliness due to cultural isolation from local students.
Availability of international clubs helps combat a sense of 'outsidership.'
Pressure builds from adapting to hierarchical lab environments.
Large cultural events offer relief and primary social bonding spaces.
Students may feel disconnected during long planning phases for high-pressure exams.
money: ¥40,000–¥80,000 monthly rent creates housing stress despite cheaper dorm options.
Scholarships provide partial relief but require academic performance validation.
Well-paying tech jobs/internships can offer high returns but need skills and interviews.
Frequent ¥500–700 train daily commutes strain budgets further.
Unexpected startup costs like key money during initial rental processes take time.
city_major_specific_challenges: Undergraduate Computer Science workloads combined with Tokyo's long commutes (often >1hr).
Humid climate strains physical stamina in summer lab schedules.
Limited programming internships outside advanced coding levels.
Mental stress from peers preferring tighter Japanese hierarchical circle closings.
